question: Let P(n) be the statement that a postage of n cents can be formed using just Ꮞ-cent stamps and 𐓒-cent stamps. The parts of this exercise outline a strong induction proof that P(n) is true for n ≥ 18. a) Show statements P(18), P(19), P(20), and P(21) are true, completing the basis step of the proof. b) What is the inductive hypothesis of the proof? What do you need to prove in the inductive step? c) Complete the inductive step for k ≥ 21. Explain why these steps show that this statement is true whenever n ≥ 18.
response: To prove that a postage of $n$ cents can be formed using 4-cent and 7-cent stamps for all $n \ge 18$, we use the principle of strong induction.a) Basis StepWe must show that the statement $P(n)$ holds for the first four cases: $n = 18, 19, 20,$ and $21$.P(18): $18 = 4 + 7 + 7$ (One 4-cent stamp, two 7-cent stamps)P(19): $19 = 4 + 4 + 4 + 7$ (Three 4-cent stamps, one 7-cent stamp)P(20): $20 = 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4$ (Five 4-cent stamps)P(21): $21 = 7 + 7 + 7$ (Three 7-cent stamps)Since we can form each of these amounts, the basis step is complete.b) Inductive Hypothesis and GoalIn strong induction, we assume the statement is true for all values from the base case up to a point $k$.Inductive Hypothesis: Assume that $P(j)$ is true for all integers $j$ such that $18 \le j \le k$, where $k \ge 21$. This means we assume we can form any postage from 18 cents up to $k$ cents.What to Prove: We need to prove that $P(k+1)$ is true—that we can form a postage of $k+1$ cents using only 4-cent and 7-cent stamps.c) Inductive StepWe want to form $k+1$ cents of postage.The Substitution: Since we know $k \ge 21$, we can look back at the case for $(k+1) - 4$.Applying the Hypothesis: Because $k \ge 21$, it follows that $(k+1) - 4 \ge 18$. According to our inductive hypothesis, $P(k-3)$ is true.The Logic: Since $P(k-3)$ is true, we can form exactly $k-3$ cents using 4-cent and 7-cent stamps. To get to $k+1$ cents, we simply add one more 4-cent stamp to that combination:$$(k - 3) + 4 = k + 1$$Conclusion: This shows $P(k+1)$ is true.Why this works for all $n \ge 18$:By proving the four base cases ($18, 19, 20, 21$), we created a "bridge." Any value $n > 21$ can be reached by repeatedly adding 4-cent stamps to one of those four base cases. Specifically, $P(22)$ is derived from $P(18)$, $P(23)$ from $P(19)$, and so on. Because the gap we jump (4 cents) is covered by the number of consecutive base cases we proved (four), no integer is left out.
